content.text: After h attention heads each produce their output, the results must be merged into a single vector before passing to the next layer. The merge strategy is simple concatenation along the feature axis: the d_v-dimensional output of each head is placed end-to-end, producing a h·d_v-dimensional vector. Because d_v = d_model / h, this restores the original d_model dimension:

attrs.alt: Diagram showing h head outputs being concatenated into a single d_model vector, then projected through W_O, alongside an efficient packed-matmul implementation

content.text: Concatenation is lossless — each head's output occupies its own dedicated block of the output vector. If the heads were instead summed or averaged, information from different heads would mix immediately and any head could cancel another. Concatenation preserves each head's representation as a distinct subspace for W_O to then combine.
Lossless; heads stay separate until W_O
After concatenation the vector is h·d_v = d_model wide. W_O ∈ ℝ^(d_model × d_model) is a learned linear map that

and projects back to d_model. Each output neuron in W_O sees all h heads simultaneously, learning to reweight their contributions. Without W_O, the h blocks would remain independent — the model could not learn cross-head interactions.

# BERT-base example: d_model=768, h=12, d_k=d_v=64, n=seq_len
import torch

batch, n, d_model, h, d_k = 1, 10, 768, 12, 64

# Each head i produces: (batch, n, d_k) = (1, 10, 64)
head_outputs = [torch.randn(batch, n, d_k) for _ in range(h)]

# Step 1: concatenate along feature dim
concatenated = torch.cat(head_outputs, dim=-1)  # (1, 10, 768)
print(concatenated.shape)   # torch.Size([1, 10, 768])

# Step 2: output projection
W_O = torch.randn(d_model, d_model)
output = concatenated @ W_O  # (1, 10, 768)
print(output.shape)          # torch.Size([1, 10, 768])
Efficient Implementation: Reshape Instead of Cat
In practice, separate lists of head outputs are never created. The projections for all heads are packed into one large matrix multiplication, and the concatenation is achieved via a
. This avoids Python loops and keeps the entire computation on GPU as a single batched kernel.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

def multi_head_attention_packed(X, W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, h, mask=None):
    """
    X:    (batch, n, d_model)
    W_Q, W_K, W_V: (d_model, d_model)  — packed for all h heads
    W_O:  (d_model, d_model)
    """
    batch, n, d_model = X.shape
    d_k = d_model // h

    # Single matmul for all heads at once
    Q = X @ W_Q  # (batch, n, d_model)
    K = X @ W_K
    V = X @ W_V

    # Reshape to expose the head dimension: (batch, h, n, d_k)
    def split(t):
        return t.view(batch, n, h, d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    Q, K, V = split(Q), split(K), split(V)

    # Batched SDPA — processes all h heads in one call
    ctx = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V,
                                         attn_mask=mask,
                                         is_causal=False)
    # ctx: (batch, h, n, d_k)

    # Concatenation = transpose + reshape
    ctx = ctx.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch, n, d_model)
    # ctx: (batch, n, h * d_k) == (batch, n, d_model)  ← the concat

    return ctx @ W_O   # (batch, n, d_model)


# Verify shapes
batch, n, d_model, h = 2, 10, 768, 12
X = torch.randn(batch, n, d_model)
WQ = torch.randn(d_model, d_model)
WK = torch.randn(d_model, d_model)
WV = torch.randn(d_model, d_model)
WO = torch.randn(d_model, d_model)

out = multi_head_attention_packed(X, WQ, WK, WV, WO, h)
print(out.shape)  # torch.Size([2, 10, 768])
It is worth stating explicitly: the
at the end is not a separate concat operation — it

the concatenation. Because the head dimension (h) and the per-head dimension (d_k) are adjacent in memory after the transpose, flattening them together is mathematically identical to placing the h vectors end-to-end. The GPU never builds a list of separate tensors.

import torch

# Demonstrate equivalence
batch, h, n, d_k = 1, 3, 4, 2
ctx = torch.randn(batch, h, n, d_k)

# Method 1: explicit torch.cat
heads = [ctx[:, i, :, :] for i in range(h)]          # list of (1, n, d_k)
explicit_cat = torch.cat(heads, dim=-1)               # (1, n, h*d_k)

# Method 2: reshape (used in practice)
reshape_cat = ctx.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch, n, h * d_k)

print(torch.allclose(explicit_cat, reshape_cat))  # True
, the tensor has shape (batch, n, h, d_k) with heads as the second-to-last axis. The
call ensures the data is laid out sequentially in memory: for each token position i, the d_k values from head 1 come first, then head 2's d_k values, and so on. The final
simply reinterprets this contiguous block as a single h·d_k-dimensional vector per position — the concatenated representation.

Concatenation preserves each head's output as a distinct block, giving W_O full control over how to blend them. The efficient packed implementation avoids Python loops entirely — all h heads run in one batched GPU kernel, and the concat is a free reshape.

Head concatenation is the mechanism that makes multi-head attention more than the sum of its parts. Without it, you would simply have h independent attention computations with no way to combine their outputs. The concatenation operation, followed by the W_O projection, is what allows different heads — each attending to different aspects of the input (syntax, coreference, positional relationships, rare word dependencies) — to contribute jointly to the next layer's representation.

The specific choice to concatenate rather than sum or average is not arbitrary. Concatenation is the only lossless merge operation: every bit of information from every head survives into the concatenated vector. W_O then learns a linear map over the full h*d_v space, giving it visibility into all heads simultaneously. This lets W_O learn cross-head interactions — for example, weighting a syntactic head more heavily when a semantic head fires strongly, a combination that would be invisible if outputs were summed.

In practice, the concatenation step is free at runtime — it is implemented as a tensor reshape, not a memory copy. The multi-head attention block adds exactly one additional matrix multiplication (W_O) beyond what single-head attention requires. This is a key efficiency advantage: multi-head attention gives h independent views of the input for only the marginal cost of W_O.
Each head's entire d_v-dimensional output is preserved. No head can be cancelled or masked out by another before W_O sees the combined representation.

Because each head operates in its own dedicated block of the concatenated vector, heads are mathematically independent before W_O. This encourages specialisation: different heads learn different pattern detectors without interfering with each other during optimisation.
W_O learns which linear combinations of heads are useful for each output neuron. This is strictly more expressive than any fixed merge strategy (sum, mean, max).

h * d_v = d_model exactly. The concatenated vector has the same dimension as the input X, making it trivially compatible with residual connections, layer norm, and the next sublayer — no shape transformation needed.
The packed matmul implementation means all h heads run as a single batched GPU operation. The reshape that performs the concatenation costs zero FLOPs.

The concatenation design forces a fixed tradeoff: d_k = d_v = d_model / h. As you increase h to get more heads (more diverse views), each head's subspace becomes narrower. With BERT-base at d_model=768 and h=12, each head operates in just 64 dimensions — enough for simple pattern detectors, but limited for complex representations. GPT-3 uses h=96 heads in its 12288-dim layers, giving d_k = 128 per head.

This tradeoff has motivated research into alternatives that decouple head count from head dimensionality. Multi-Query Attention (MQA) and Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) allow key/value heads to be shared across multiple query heads, reducing KV cache memory without proportionally reducing model quality. But all variants still use concatenation (or its reshape equivalent) as the final merge step.

In GQA (used in Llama-2-70B with g=8 groups), the concatenation after the value projection is performed within each group: each query head attends to the group's shared V, and the h query-head outputs are still concatenated end-to-end before W_O. The concatenation mechanism is unchanged; only the V matrix is shared.
Increasing head count (more heads, smaller d_k):
Adds representational diversity — more heads can specialise independently.
Risks: each head has fewer dimensions to work with; may converge to redundant heads.
Standard practice: 8–32 heads for most architectures.
Decreasing head count (fewer heads, larger d_k):
Each head can capture more complex patterns in its larger subspace.
Less diversity across heads — the model may miss some relationship types.
Used in: single-head attention as a baseline, lightweight models.
Choosing between MHA, MQA, GQA (KV sharing):
MHA: use when training from scratch and KV cache is not a bottleneck (encoder models).
GQA: use for long-context generative models where KV cache dominates memory (Llama-2-70B, Mistral).
MQA: use for the most extreme memory savings (PaLM, Falcon — aggressive memory optimisation).
Real-World Example: Implementing a Transformer Block from Scratch
A complete, self-contained transformer block that demonstrates head concatenation, the W_O projection, residual connection, and layer norm — the exact sequence used in every production transformer.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """
    Standard multi-head attention with packed matmuls.
    Head concatenation is performed via reshape — no explicit cat() call.

    Parameters match BERT-base: d_model=768, num_heads=12.
    """

    def __init__(self, d_model: int = 768, num_heads: int = 12, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0, f"{d_model} must be divisible by {num_heads}"

        self.d_model = d_model
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads  # 64 for BERT-base

        # Packed projections: W_Q, W_K, W_V each have shape (d_model, d_model)
        # This packs h heads' weights into one matrix for efficiency.
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_O = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

        self.attn_dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
        self.scale = math.sqrt(self.d_k)

    def split_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """(batch, seq, d_model) → (batch, h, seq, d_k)"""
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        return x.view(B, T, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def merge_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        (batch, h, seq, d_k) → (batch, seq, d_model)

        THIS IS THE CONCATENATION:
        transpose(1, 2) → (batch, seq, h, d_k)
        .contiguous().view(..., d_model) → (batch, seq, h * d_k)
        Lays each head's d_k values sequentially in memory — identical to
        torch.cat([head_i for head_i in x], dim=-1) but zero-copy.
        """
        B, _, T, _ = x.shape
        return x.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, self.d_model)

    def forward(
        self,
        query: torch.Tensor,         # (B, T_q, d_model)
        key: torch.Tensor,           # (B, T_k, d_model)
        value: torch.Tensor,         # (B, T_k, d_model)
        mask: torch.Tensor = None,   # (B, 1, T_q, T_k) or (B, h, T_q, T_k)
    ) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:

        # Project and split into h heads
        Q = self.split_heads(self.W_Q(query))   # (B, h, T_q, d_k)
        K = self.split_heads(self.W_K(key))     # (B, h, T_k, d_k)
        V = self.split_heads(self.W_V(value))   # (B, h, T_k, d_k)

        # Scaled dot-product attention for all heads simultaneously
        attn_scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) / self.scale  # (B, h, T_q, T_k)
        if mask is not None:
            attn_scores = attn_scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9)
        attn_weights = self.attn_dropout(F.softmax(attn_scores, dim=-1))  # (B, h, T_q, T_k)

        ctx = attn_weights @ V   # (B, h, T_q, d_k)

        # Concatenation + output projection
        concat = self.merge_heads(ctx)  # (B, T_q, d_model)  ← the concat step
        output = self.W_O(concat)       # (B, T_q, d_model)

        return output, attn_weights


class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    """One transformer layer: MHA + FFN, both with residual + LayerNorm."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int = 768, num_heads: int = 12, d_ff: int = 3072, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = MultiHeadAttention(d_model, num_heads, dropout)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
        )
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, mask: torch.Tensor = None) -> torch.Tensor:
        # Pre-norm variant (used in GPT-2 and most modern transformers)
        attn_out, _ = self.attn(self.norm1(x), self.norm1(x), self.norm1(x), mask)
        x = x + self.drop(attn_out)      # residual connection after attention
        x = x + self.drop(self.ffn(self.norm2(x)))   # residual after FFN
        return x


# Verify shapes with BERT-base dimensions
block = TransformerBlock(d_model=768, num_heads=12, d_ff=3072)
x = torch.randn(2, 16, 768)         # batch=2, seq=16, d_model=768
out = block(x)
print(out.shape)   # torch.Size([2, 16, 768]) — shape preserved by design

After .transpose(), tensor memory is non-contiguous. Calling .view() on a non-contiguous tensor raises a RuntimeError. Always call .contiguous() first, or use .reshape() which handles this automatically.
Applying dropout after softmax instead of on attention weights:
Dropout on attention weights (the standard Transformer approach) randomly zeros out entire attention paths, acting as a regulariser. Dropout after the value-weighted sum is less effective.
Using the wrong d_k in the scale factor:

The scaling factor is 1/sqrt(d_k) where d_k is the per-head key dimension (d_model / h), NOT d_model. Using d_model produces vastly underscaled attention scores that collapse to uniform distributions.
Not masking padding tokens in bidirectional attention:
In BERT-style models, padding tokens (token_id=0) should be masked so they don't contribute to attention. Pass a padding mask that sets those positions to -inf before softmax.

import torch, torch.nn.functional as F

# WRONG: missing .contiguous() — may raise RuntimeError
ctx = torch.randn(2, 12, 10, 64)  # (batch, h, seq, d_k)
ctx_transposed = ctx.transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, seq, h, d_k) — non-contiguous
# ctx_transposed.view(2, 10, 768)  # ← RuntimeError in many PyTorch versions

# CORRECT option 1: .contiguous().view()
merged = ctx.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(2, 10, 768)

# CORRECT option 2: .reshape() (handles non-contiguous automatically)
merged = ctx.transpose(1, 2).reshape(2, 10, 768)

# WRONG: scale factor uses d_model instead of d_k
d_model, h = 768, 12
d_k = d_model // h  # = 64
Q = torch.randn(2, h, 10, d_k)
K = torch.randn(2, h, 10, d_k)

scores_wrong = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) / (d_model ** 0.5)  # scale by sqrt(768) — too large
scores_right = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) / (d_k ** 0.5)      # scale by sqrt(64) = 8

print("Wrong scale mean logit:", scores_wrong.abs().mean().item())   # ~0.35 — underscaled
print("Correct scale mean logit:", scores_right.abs().mean().item()) # ~2.8 — appropriate scale
This fused kernel handles scaling, softmax, and attention dropout in a single CUDA call. It automatically uses FlashAttention when available, reducing memory from O(n²) to O(n) during training.
Use GQA for long-context or memory-constrained deployments:
For generative models where KV cache is the memory bottleneck (long sequences, large batch sizes), GQA reduces KV cache by h/g times with minimal quality degradation.
Initialise W_O from a scaled normal distribution:
The original Transformer paper uses Xavier initialisation. GPT-2 additionally scales W_O by 1/sqrt(num_layers) to prevent residual stream explosion in deep networks.

Compute the average attention entropy per head. Heads with near-uniform attention (high entropy) or single-token attention (near-zero entropy) are likely redundant. Head pruning can remove 20–30% of heads with negligible quality loss.

import torch, torch.nn.functional as F

# Best practice: use PyTorch's fused SDPA (handles FlashAttention automatically)
def efficient_multi_head_attention(X, W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, h, mask=None, training=False):
    B, T, d_model = X.shape
    d_k = d_model // h

    Q = (X @ W_Q).view(B, T, h, d_k).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, h, T, d_k)
    K = (X @ W_K).view(B, T, h, d_k).transpose(1, 2)
    V = (X @ W_V).view(B, T, h, d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    # Fused kernel: scales, masks, softmax, dropout, matmul in one CUDA call
    ctx = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(
        Q, K, V,
        attn_mask=mask,
        dropout_p=0.1 if training else 0.0,
        is_causal=False,
    )  # (B, h, T, d_k)

    # Concatenation via reshape
    out = ctx.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, d_model)
    return out @ W_O   # (B, T, d_model)


# GPT-2-style W_O initialisation with depth scaling
import math
def init_weights(module, num_layers: int):
    if isinstance(module, torch.nn.Linear):
        std = 0.02
        if hasattr(module, '_is_output_projection'):
            std /= math.sqrt(2 * num_layers)  # scale down residual projections
        torch.nn.init.normal_(module.weight, mean=0.0, std=std)
        if module.bias is not None:
            torch.nn.init.zeros_(module.bias)

The dominant cost of multi-head attention is the attention matrix computation — O(n²d) where n is sequence length and d is per-head dimension. This quadratic scaling in sequence length is the primary scalability bottleneck for long-context models. The concatenation and W_O projection are O(nd²), which is linear in n.

FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) rewrites the attention kernel to fuse all operations (scale, mask, softmax, dropout, weighted sum) into a single pass over the KV blocks, keeping the intermediate n×n attention matrix in SRAM rather than HBM. This reduces memory from O(n²) to O(n) and achieves 2–4x speedup on A100 GPUs. PyTorch 2.0's F.scaled_dot_product_attention uses FlashAttention automatically when tensors are on CUDA.

attrs.latex: \text{FLOPs for one MHA layer} = 4 \cdot n \cdot d_{\text{model}}^2 + 2 \cdot n^2 \cdot d_{\text{model}}

content.text: The first term (4nd²) comes from Q, K, V, and W_O projections. The second term (2n²d) comes from the attention score computation and value aggregation. For short sequences (n < d_model), the projection FLOPs dominate; for long sequences (n > d_model), the attention FLOPs dominate and quadratic scaling becomes expensive.
Reference PyTorch implementation; use for learning, override for production
Fused kernel with optional FlashAttention; use in production
pip install flash-attn; 5–8x speedup for long sequences on Ampere/Hopper GPUs
Memory-efficient attention variants; drop-in replacements for standard attention
GPU kernel language; used to implement custom attention variants
Automatically replaces attention blocks with optimised kernels at inference time
Comparison: Concatenation vs. Alternative Merge Strategies

The choice of head merge strategy has been studied empirically. Concatenation followed by a learned linear projection consistently outperforms alternatives, but the research literature has explored other approaches:
Can be seen as W_O fixed to identity
Same as sum but scaled; dilutes signal
Information loss; non-differentiable in some settings
Sparse routing across heads; complex training

The sum-then-project view is instructive: summing all head outputs and then multiplying by W_O is equivalent to multiplying each head's output by a separate row slice of W_O — which is exactly what concatenation-then-project computes. The difference is that concatenation keeps the slices separate until W_O, preserving structure; summation mixes them immediately and loses per-head identity.
Head concatenation itself introduces no direct security vulnerabilities, but the multi-head attention mechanism it enables has reliability implications that matter in production.

Individual heads can degenerate during training — some heads learn to attend to the separator token uniformly (an 'ignored' pattern), contributing nothing to the concatenated representation. Monitor head entropy during training to detect and potentially prune degenerate heads.

The W_O projection amplifies any numerical instability in the concatenated head outputs. In INT8 or INT4 quantisation, the W_O layer should be calibrated carefully — quantisation errors here propagate directly to the next layer's residual stream.

Without the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling, attention logits grow with d_k, causing softmax to saturate at extreme values and producing near-zero gradients. Always verify the scale factor matches d_k, especially when modifying architectures.

If padding positions receive -inf in the mask and all positions in a sequence are padded (empty input), softmax produces NaN rather than uniform attention. Guard against empty sequences in preprocessing.
Head concatenation is a lossless merge:
each head's d_v-dimensional output occupies its own block in the concatenated d_model-dimensional vector, preserving all per-head information for W_O to learn from.
In practice, concatenation is implemented as a
: .transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch, seq, d_model) — no memory copy, no extra FLOPs.
it is a learned linear map over the full concatenated space that can compute arbitrary linear combinations of all heads, enabling cross-head interactions impossible with fixed merge strategies.

content: means that as h increases, each head's subspace becomes narrower. This tradeoff motivates GQA (shared KV) and other variants for large-h architectures.

content.text: for fused computation that automatically uses FlashAttention on CUDA, reducing attention memory from O(n²) to O(n).
FlashAttention does not change the mathematical output
— it computes the same attention and the same concatenation, just in a hardware-efficient tiled order without materialising the full attention matrix.
— degenerate heads (uniform or single-token attention) can be pruned with negligible quality loss and reduce inference cost by reducing effective h.
